back_translation: As for Saul, he continued to threaten to kill the disciples of the Lord without letup. So he went in to the high priest
and requested letters from him to the synagogues in Damascus, so that if he found any men or women who belonged to "the Way," he could bind and bring them to Jerusalem.
While he was traveling and was nearly arriving at the city of Damascus, suddenly there was a light from heaven that shone around him.
He fell to the ground and heard a voice speaking to him, "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?"
Saul asked respectfully, "Who are you, O Lord?" And he said in reply, "I am Jesus whom you are persecuting."
"But get up and enter the city, and someone will tell you what you must do."
As for the men who were traveling with Saul, they stood there stunned and speechless, hearing a sound but seeing no one.
Saul got up from the ground, but even though he opened his eyes he could not see anything. So they led him by the hand into the city of Damascus.
He could not see for three days, and he did not eat or drink.
In the city of Damascus there was a disciple named Ananias. The Lord spoke to him in a vision, "Ananias!" He replied, "I, your servant, am here, O Lord."
The Lord then said to him, "Get up and go to the street named 'Straight Street' and ask for a man from Tarsus named Saul at the house of Judas. Behold, he is praying,
and he has seen in a vision a man named Ananias coming in and laying hands on him so that he would see again."
Ananias replied respectfully, "O Lord, I have heard from many people about this man, how much harm he has done to your saints in Jerusalem.
And here he has authority from the chief priests to bind all those who call on your name."
The Lord said to him, "Go, because this man is a vessel that we have chosen to carry our name before the Gentiles, the kings, and the people of Israel.
For we ourselves will reveal to him how much he must suffer for the sake of our name."

So Ananias went and entered into that house. Then he laid hands on Saul and said, "Saul, O brother, the Lord — Jesus who appeared to you on the road where you were coming — has sent me so that you may see again and be filled with the Holy Spirit."
Immediately there was something that looked like scales falling off from his eyes. He regained his sight and got up and received baptism.
After he had eaten, he regained strength. He stayed with the disciples in the city of Damascus for several days.
Immediately he began to proclaim about Jesus in the synagogues, saying, "He is the Son of God."

Everyone who heard was stunned and said to one another, "Isn't this the man who used to destroy those who call on this name in Jerusalem? Hasn't he come here in order to bind them and take them to the chief priests?"
As for Saul, he grew all the stronger and confounded the Jews who lived in the city of Damascus, by proving that Jesus is the Christ.
When many days had passed, the Jews conspired together to kill him.
But their plan became known to Saul. They watched the city gates day and night in order to kill him.
But his disciples took him at night and lowered him in a basket through the city wall.
When Saul arrived in Jerusalem, he tried to join the disciples, but everyone feared him, not believing that he was a disciple.

But Barnabas brought Saul to the apostles and told them how Saul had seen the Lord on the road and how he had spoken to him, and also told them how he had proclaimed boldly in the city of Damascus in the name of Jesus.
So Saul stayed with them, moving about in and out freely in Jerusalem and proclaiming boldly in the name of the Lord.
He conversed and debated with the Jews who spoke Greek, but they tried to kill him.
When the brothers learned of this, they took him down to the city of Caesarea and sent him to the city of Tarsus.

At that time the church throughout the regions of Judea, Galilee, and Samaria had peace. It received edification and walked in the fear of the Lord, and multiplied in number through the encouragement of the Holy Spirit.
As Peter traveled throughout every place, he went down to visit the saints who lived in the city of Lydda.
There he met a man named Aeneas who was paralyzed and had been bedridden for eight years already.
Peter said to him, "Aeneas! Jesus Christ heals you and makes you well. Get up and put away your bedding yourself." Immediately he got up.
Everyone who lived in the city of Lydda and on the plain of Sharon saw Aeneas, and they turned back to the Lord.
In the city of Joppa there was a female disciple named Tabitha, which translates to "Dorcas." She was always full of doing good and giving alms.
At that very time she fell ill and passed away. They washed her corpse and placed her in the upstairs room.
Since the city of Lydda was near the city of Joppa, the disciples heard that Peter was there, and they sent two men to him, saying respectfully, "Please do not delay to come to us."

Peter got up and went with them. When he arrived, they brought him upstairs to the upper room. The widows came and stood around him, weeping and bringing out the tunics and clothing that Dorcas used to make while she was still with them to show him.
Peter had everyone go out of the room, then knelt down and prayed. When he had turned to her corpse, he said, "Tabitha, get up." She opened her eyes, and when she saw Peter, she sat up.
Peter extended his hand for her to grasp and helped her stand up. Then he called the saints together with the widows in, and presented her in a living state.
This event became famous throughout the city of Joppa, and many people believed in the Lord.
Peter stayed in the city of Joppa for many days with a certain Simon the leather-tanner.
